transformation_type: alcohol-derived nitrite ester to γ-hydroxy oxime or related remote-functionalized product
mechanism_type: oxygen-centered radical generation followed by 1,5-H abstraction and NO capture
reactant_pattern_text: aliphatic alcohol converted to nitrite ester under thermal or photolytic conditions
product_pattern_text: γ-hydroxy oxime, remote oxidized alcohol/oxime, or radical-trapping product after nitrosyl capture
common_conditions: nitrite ester generation then thermolysis or photolysis to induce remote radical transposition and oxime formation
description_short: The Barton nitrite ester reaction enables remote C-H oxidation or radical functionalization through photolysis or thermolysis of nitrite esters.

transformation_type: carboxylic acid to alkane or substituted radical-derived product via thiohydroxamate ester
mechanism_type: AIBN-initiated radical chain with decarboxylation of acyl-derived radical precursor
reactant_pattern_text: carboxylic acid converted to acid chloride and then to thiohydroxamate ester
product_pattern_text: decarboxylated product bearing hydrogen or other radical-trapped substituent after CO2 extrusion
common_conditions: preformation of Barton ester followed by radical initiation and hydrogen-atom transfer chemistry
description_short: The Barton radical decarboxylation removes carboxyl groups after conversion to thiohydroxamate esters under tin-hydride radical conditions.

transformation_type: alcohol to alkane through thiocarbonyl derivative under tin-hydride radical conditions
mechanism_type: radical chain reduction of xanthate or related thiocarbonyl derivatives
reactant_pattern_text: secondary or tertiary alcohol converted to xanthate, thiocarbonylimidazolide, or thioester derivative
product_pattern_text: deoxygenated hydrocarbon with OH replaced by hydrogen
common_conditions: alcohol derivatization to a thiocarbonyl surrogate followed by refluxing radical deoxygenation
description_short: The Barton-McCombie reaction removes alcohol oxygen after conversion to a thiocarbonyl derivative and radical reduction with tributyltin hydride.

transformation_type: activated alkene plus aldehyde or imine to allylic alcohol adduct
mechanism_type: tertiary amine or phosphine catalyzed Michael addition / aldol-type coupling
reactant_pattern_text: activated alkene such as acrylate or enone with aldehyde or imine under tertiary amine/phosphine catalysis
product_pattern_text: densely functionalized allylic alcohol or related Baylis-Hillman adduct
common_conditions: organocatalytic coupling often slow but highly atom-economic, with asymmetric variants using cinchona alkaloids or BINAP-derived catalysts
description_short: The Baylis-Hillman reaction forms C-C bonds between activated alkenes and aldehydes or imines under tertiary amine or phosphine catalysis to give allylic alcohol adducts.

transformation_type: oxime to amide or lactam under acid activation with anti migration
mechanism_type: electrophilic activation of oxime followed by 1,2-shift to nitrilium ion and hydrolysis
reactant_pattern_text: aldoxime or ketoxime treated with Brønsted acid, Lewis acid, sulfonylating agent, or related activator
product_pattern_text: amide, lactam, or nitrogen heterocycle derived from rearranged nitrilium intermediate
common_conditions: anti-oriented substituent migrates upon oxime activation to give lactams or amides; often used for cyclic ketone ring expansion
description_short: The Beckmann rearrangement converts oximes into amides or lactams through activation of the oxime and anti migratory rearrangement via nitrilium ions.
